title: What Is the Answer to These Questions?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The source prints boxed repetitions of three central questions concerning the Load Flow Study, the Madhya Pradesh tariff comparison, and the extension of the preferential tariff. They are preserved in their printed positions. The scans also confirm that the preferential tariff initially ran through `31-3-2015`, correcting the OCR form `21-3-2015`. The Tamil reading copy below repairs only such unambiguous source readings; the canonical Tamil source remains unchanged. The allegations in this letter remain attributed to Kalaignar's questions, quoted correspondence, court proceedings, and named public statements.
paragraphs: I explained in detail the extent of the alleged corruption and irregularities in purchasing coal for the Electricity Board. To date, no reply of any kind has come from either the Chief Minister or the minister. Even if they do not answer now, after the election they will have to answer when a commission of inquiry summons them and asks about these allegations. That concerns the coal scandal.

I now set out the allegation that the Tamil Nadu Government suffered a loss of Rs 7,576 crore in the purchase of solar power by the same Electricity Board. Watch for yourselves whether the ruling side gives even this an answer!

The Tamil Nadu Generation and Distribution Corporation, TANGEDCO, invited tenders to establish solar power plants with a total generating capacity of 3,000 megawatts. The tender period was fixed from 5 December 2012 to 2 January 2013. The final date for receiving and opening the tenders was fixed as 4 January 2013.

From the tenders received, TANGEDCO selected fifty-two. It sent letters to those fifty-two contractors agreeing that they would generate and supply 708 megawatts of electricity at Rs 6.48 per unit, with a 5 per cent annual price increase for ten years from 1 April 2014. After all these proceedings had been completed, however, the matter was frozen without any decision being taken.

The minister for the department himself had said that no agreement could be concluded with solar-power producers without conducting a **Load Flow Study** and satisfying the land requirements. When those requirements had not been met, was it proper for TANGEDCO to conclude an agreement with the Adani Group on 4 July 2015 in the presence of Chief Minister Jayalalithaa?

Meanwhile, on 12 September 2014, the Tamil Nadu Electricity Regulatory Commission issued an order concerning the overall price and procedures by which TANGEDCO would purchase electricity from solar-power producers. Producers who approached first were to receive priority. The fixed price, described as a preferential tariff, was set at Rs 7.01 per unit and was to remain in force until 31 March 2015.

TANGEDCO sought the Commission's approval for power-purchase agreements made with selected solar producers. In an order dated 15 September 2014, the Commission rejected them because the agreements had not followed the Union Government's guidelines. TANGEDCO then decided to continue with the same contractors at the Rs 7.01 per-unit rate fixed by the Commission and wrote to all contractors asking them to move to the preferential tariff.

From the moment the Tamil Nadu Government signed an agreement with Adani in July 2015 to purchase 648 megawatts of solar power, Opposition parties raised various allegations about the project. A new controversy, it was said, lent support to those allegations.
As a general rule, five acres of land are required to generate one megawatt of solar power. Accordingly, Adani would need to possess 3,240 acres to generate 648 megawatts.

Under TANGEDCO's proceedings dated 7 October 2014, a preliminary decision could be made only after completion of the Load Flow Study, payment of the security deposit and fulfilment of the land requirement. Had the Adani Group fulfilled all these requirements? The letter says it had not.
Had TANGEDCO certified that the Adani Group had acquired the required land?

The minister for the department himself had said that no agreement could be concluded with solar-power producers without conducting a **Load Flow Study** and satisfying the land requirements. When those requirements had not been met, was it proper for TANGEDCO to conclude an agreement with the Adani Group on 4 July 2015 in the presence of Chief Minister Jayalalithaa?

On 4 July 2015, the Tamil Nadu Government signed an agreement with the Adani Group, valued at Rs 4,536 crore, to purchase electricity for twenty-five years at Rs 7.01 per unit. Allegations arose when the Madhya Pradesh Government finalised a solar-power purchase agreement just two weeks later. The same Adani company had offered to supply Madhya Pradesh with solar power at Rs 6.04 per unit. Is it true or not that Madhya Pradesh rejected Adani's bid and instead approved the purchase of power for twenty-five years from SkyPower South East Asia, a Mauritius-based company, at Rs 5.05 per unit?

Adani began purchasing land in Ramanathapuram district for a solar park capable of producing 648 megawatts. Complaints alleged numerous irregularities in those purchases, including that intermediaries bought land cheaply from farmers and resold it to Adani at higher prices.

A case filed by Adani in the Madras High Court a few months earlier was said to lend support to those allegations. Adani had appointed an advocate named Kabilan to verify land documents. On Adani's behalf, an injunction was obtained from the High Court restraining Kabilan from disclosing any information concerning the company. When the background was examined, the letter says, several notable details emerged.

Advocate Kabilan was the proprietor of Kabilan Associates, a law firm operating from Anna Salai in Chennai. He was appointed to verify documents for land bought by Adani in Ramanathapuram district. His fee was fixed at Rs 6,000 per acre, on the basis of which Adani paid him Rs 70 lakh. Kabilan later wrote to Adani seeking Rs 15,000 per acre. He allegedly said that if the company failed to pay, he would disclose information about its solar parks and that such disclosure could force Adani to abandon the project in Tamil Nadu.
The letter quotes Kabilan's communication to Adani as alleging:

> “Various illegal acts took place in the purchase of land for the solar park. Various unlawful documents were submitted under the agreement made with the Tamil Nadu Electricity Board for the purchase of electricity. It is noteworthy that the Tamil Nadu Government entered into the agreement to purchase electricity from Adani at Rs 7.01 per unit on the basis of these unlawful documents.”

It further states that, on the strength of this agreement, Adani obtained loans of Rs 2,303.98 crore from IndusInd Bank, Rs 1,000 crore from the State Bank of India, Rs 500 crore from Yes Bank and Rs 803.98 crore from Standard Chartered Bank.

Kabilan's quoted letter alleged that various irregularities had also occurred in obtaining those loans and that SEBI rules, contractual rules and other requirements had been violated. It described the cited violations as only a drop in the ocean. It claimed that if the Tamil Nadu Electricity Board, other government departments, public-interest litigants and the media learned the information, Adani's agreements with the Tamil Nadu Government could face cancellation. It further claimed that even a preliminary inquiry would reveal how Adani had deceived the Electricity Board.

Nagalasamy, then a member of the Electricity Regulatory Commission, said when the agreement was made that it would cause the Tamil Nadu Government a loss of Rs 25,000 crore over the next twenty-five years. Yet, disregarding every objection, including those from the Opposition, the Tamil Nadu Government continued to implement it.
In a statement on the matter, I had said:

> “This case shows that the AIADMK Government hurriedly made this agreement with the Adani Group and, contrary to law, unlawfully and fraudulently allotted the power project to that group without calling for any tender. A lawful criminal investigation must therefore be conducted into the AIADMK Government's award of this project, the concealed facts must be brought out, and the people must be given a clear account.”

When solar power was available in States such as Madhya Pradesh at Rs 5.05 per unit, why did TANGEDCO neither appeal to the Appellate Tribunal against the higher price fixed in Tamil Nadu nor challenge that price before the High Court?
On 16 June 2015, I was asked: “Is the Adani Group entering into an agreement with the Government to establish a solar-power plant valued at Rs 1,400 crore?” I replied:

> “Reports say that permission has been granted to produce 200 megawatts of solar power in Tamil Nadu through a project valued at Rs 1,400 crore. Negotiations are reportedly under way with the AIADMK Government to increase that 200 megawatts to 1,000 megawatts. If some people say there is some connection between the temporary relief Jayalalithaa obtained in the appeal in the disproportionate-assets case and Adani's solar-power agreement, we do not know what truth there may be in it!”

PMK founder Dr S. Ramadoss had stated that, as the cost of establishing solar plants had fallen, the purchase price of solar power in the current year had dropped to Rs 5.86 and could fall to about Rs 5 per unit the following year. In that situation, he said, the Government had entered into an agreement with Adani to buy power at Rs 7.01 per unit, roughly Rs 2 more.

When the Tamil Nadu Electricity Regulatory Commission's order dated 12 September 2014 fixed the preferential tariff of Rs 7.01 until 31 March 2015, why did TANGEDCO challenge it neither before the Appellate Tribunal nor before the High Court?

Is it true or not that the Tamil Nadu Electricity Regulatory Commission, comprising Akshayakumar and G. Rajagopal, members favoured by the minister, unilaterally extended the Rs 7.01 preferential tariff from 1 April 2015 to 31 March 2016? Who instructed them to extend the period and issue that unilateral order?

When solar power was available in States such as Madhya Pradesh at Rs 5.05 per unit, why did TANGEDCO neither appeal to the Appellate Tribunal against the higher price fixed in Tamil Nadu nor challenge that price before the High Court?

Next, the AIADMK Government invited fresh tenders for construction of the Udangudi thermal power station. The Southern Central China Power Energy Designing Institute-TRESE consortium filed a petition in the High Court saying that although it had submitted the lowest bid, its bid was rejected. It sought an injunction against the retender. Justice Sathyanarayanan issued an interim order restraining the issue of a new tender and directed that no fresh tender be invited until the case concluded.
In those circumstances, was it proper to invite tenders for a second time to construct the Udangudi thermal power station? Whom was this second tender meant to deceive?

Is it true or not that the Tamil Nadu Electricity Regulatory Commission, comprising Akshayakumar and G. Rajagopal, members favoured by the minister, unilaterally extended the Rs 7.01 preferential tariff from 1 April 2015 to 31 March 2016? Who instructed them to extend the period and issue that unilateral order?

Did the Commission's other member, Nagalasamy, issue an order on 1 April 2015 stating that this decision was unlawful and unnecessary, that it favoured solar-power producers, and that it would cause a major loss to the public treasury? What action was taken on it? Minister Natham Viswanathan now says it was the Commission's independent decision. Should he not have acted against that decision?
When States such as Madhya Pradesh bought solar power for Rs 2 less per unit than Tamil Nadu, why did Tamil Nadu pay the higher price?
If 947 megawatts are purchased at this price, will the public treasury suffer a loss of Rs 7,576 crore, or will it not?
What is the answer to all these questions?
With affection,
M.K.
